weekly_brief_draft:
## Lead Signal

This week is a null signal week for the FIMI and Cognitive Warfare Monitor. Across the May 9 to 15 window, the interpreter stage identified no new foreign information manipulation and interference operations, no fresh coordinated inauthentic behaviour disclosures, and no government attributions or major institutional publications within the Tier 1 to 3 corpus. The information integrity composite remains stable rather than improved, and the absence of new cases reflects a structural reporting gap rather than a reduction in hostile activity.

The lead signal is that exhaustive review of the main platform and institutional sources produced no new disclosures dated within the weekly window. Meta coordinated inauthentic behaviour reporting, Google Threat Analysis Group bulletins, Microsoft MSTIC and MTAC posts, EEAS EUvsDisinfo outputs, and research by Stanford Internet Observatory, DFRLab, ASPI, and Bellingcat yielded no new FIMI specific publications in this period. The most recent Tier 1 primary materials that continue to anchor this monitor remain Meta s H1 2026 Adversarial Threat Report from March 11 2026 and Google TAG s Q4 2025 bulletin from January 29 2026, both outside this week s temporal scope. The interpreter assesses that Russia, China, and Iran remain highly active or active as FIMI actors on the basis of these prior baselines, so the present quiet in disclosures is read as a function of quarterly or semi annual reporting cadence rather than a pause in operations.

## Other Developments

The most consequential development this week is in fact a non development: the persistence of a disclosure cadence gap that creates a structural blind spot in FIMI monitoring. The absence of new Tier 1 platform disclosures for more than two months, with Meta s last CIB report published on March 11 and Google TAG s last bulletin on January 29, sustains a situation in which known highly active actors are not matched by commensurate public transparency. This gap is described in the interpreter analysis as a governance choice rather than a technical constraint, given that platforms retain real time detection capabilities but publish on institutional timelines that emphasise legal review and risk management. The information integrity composite highlights platform transparency as the weakest component, with a score of 0.40, and explicitly links that weakness to the nine week Meta gap and fifteen week Google TAG gap.

Actor posture tracking this week shows continuity rather than change. Russia remains described as a highly active multi platform operator targeting the post Soviet periphery, Western Europe, and Ukraine related narratives, with persistent activity despite repeated platform disruptions and no new disruption disclosed in the current window. China is characterised as highly active through high volume spam and pivot models such as Spamouflage or Taizi Flood that target narratives around United States China relations, Tibet, and Taiwan, with increasing sophistication noted in prior Meta reporting and no new platform disclosures this week. Iran is assessed as active through cyber enabled influence operations associated with IUVM and Cotton Sandstorm, with the Endless Mayfly attribution to IUVM confirmed in earlier Meta reporting and no new corroboration or disruption this week. For Gulf, United States, and Israeli origin operators, the interpreter emphasises that the absence of new disclosure reflects a structural coverage gap driven by institutional perimeter design, commercial relationships, and domestic legal constraints, rather than evidence of reduced operational tempo.

The campaign tracker highlights the continuing relevance of several previously disclosed operations even in the absence of new casework. The Russian Doppelganger operation remains assessed as active and uses fake news sites that spoof legitimate media outlets combined with multi platform amplification across services such as Meta, X, and Telegram, targeting European Union member states and broader Western audiences. The latest underlying documentation for Doppelganger comes from Meta and Google TAG, and the interpreter notes that there has been no new disclosure this week, with last activity dated March 11 2026. The Endless Mayfly or IUVM campaign remains live in the baseline, with Meta s H1 2026 report updating attribution for this persistent Iranian operation to the Iran International Union of Virtual Media and documenting English language targeting of Western audiences, while separate Iranian activity was disrupted in Q4 2025. There has been no new activity for this operation in the present week, and the attribution remains based on a single Tier 1 platform source with MF2 and MF3 flags indicating limited independent corroboration and lack of fresh primary verification. The Portal Kombat network of Russia linked domains blocked by Google TAG, which targeted Western Europe via YouTube and involved fifty seven domains in the initial action, is also carried forward with no new disclosure since January 2026 and with MF3 indicating reliance on earlier reporting.

Risk indicators this week are dominated by structural factors rather than incident driven spikes. Platform Opacity remains rated Elevated, with an amber status, because there were no new Tier 1 platform disclosures in the May 9 to 15 window and because Meta and Google TAG remain on nine week and fifteen week disclosure gaps respectively while X or Twitter still lacks any CIB equivalent transparency mechanism and has an estimated twenty week gap in reporting. The Attribution Gap vector is also rated Elevated and focuses on structural underdocumentation of Gulf, United States, and Israeli origin FIMI operations that meet DISARM criteria but do not appear in EEAS FIMI Explorer due to institutional perimeter design and constraints on platform transparency. Narrative Persistence is rated Moderate, reflecting the twelve week persistence of the Doppelganger operation despite takedowns. Amplification Reach, AI enabled FIMI, and the Regulatory Enforcement Gap are all characterised as unchanged relative to previous weeks, with the last showing that Digital Services Act Article 40 FIMI provisions are in force but that no FIMI related DSA fines or Article 40 transparency reports have yet been issued.

## Cross Monitor Connections

Even in a null signal week, cross monitor linkages remain visible through ongoing campaigns and structural risk patterns. For the european-strategic-autonomy monitor, the Doppelganger operation is particularly relevant. It explicitly targets European Union member states and wider Western audiences using spoofed media domains and multi platform amplification, and it continues to illustrate how hostile influence operations intersect with European resilience, media ecosystems, and electoral contexts. The campaign s persistence over at least twelve weeks despite documented takedowns underscores how platform governance gaps can interact with European strategic autonomy concerns.

For the conflict-escalation monitor, the Portal Kombat operation provides a continuing connection. This Russia linked network, which Google TAG blocked after identifying fifty seven domains, targeted Western Europe in several languages on YouTube with pro Russia narratives that align with broader hybrid operations around the war in Ukraine and associated security crises. The fact that last documented activity dates to January 2026 and that there has been no new bulletin since then again reflects disclosure cadence rather than a conclusion about operational cessation, and it suggests that conflict related information operations can continue to evolve between episodic transparency reports.

The ai-governance monitor is connected through the AI enabled FIMI risk vector and associated capability watch, even though no new AI specific deployments were documented this week. The interpreter notes that assessment of synthetic media and AI assisted content production depends heavily on platform willingness to disclose detection methods or on civil society research, and that no such disclosures occurred in this window. This leaves a widening gap between real world capability deployment and public attribution in the AI domain. The european-strategic-autonomy and environmental-risks monitors are also indirectly linked via the Digital Services Act framework, since DSA Article 34 and Article 40 obligations on systemic risk and information sharing with regulators are already in force yet no FIMI specific transparency reports or fines have been recorded, which constrains the European Union s ability to shape platform behaviour on climate and other policy narratives.

Cross monitor connections also arise from the concentration of attribution capacity on a narrow set of actors. The amplification and power structures module notes that attribution capacity is concentrating on Russia, China, and Iran due to institutional monitoring focus, while Gulf, United States, and Israeli operations remain structurally underdocumented. This pattern has implications for the democratic-integrity monitor, which tracks electoral manipulation, because it indicates that election related operations tied to underdocumented actors may be underrepresented in public datasets. It also matters for the macro-monitor, which observes economic coercion narratives, since gaps in attribution for some states could obscure coordinated campaigns around sanctions, trade disputes, and critical infrastructure policy.

## Outlook

The outlook for the coming week is shaped less by expected new campaigns than by the conditions under which they would become visible. A material change in this monitor would most likely come from one of three sources: a new Meta coordinated inauthentic behaviour report, a fresh Google TAG bulletin, or a significant Microsoft MSTIC or MTAC disclosure that ties technical intrusion work to information operations. Any of these would tighten the current nine week to fifteen week disclosure gaps and provide updated insight into Russian, Chinese, Iranian, or other actor behaviour. In parallel, any first enforcement action or dedicated transparency report under Digital Services Act Article 40 focused on FIMI would shift the regulatory enforcement gap by moving obligations into the realm of tangible sanctions and reporting baselines.

In the absence of such triggers, the monitor expects continued reliance on existing baselines. For Russia, China, and Iran this means that earlier 2025 and early 2026 disclosures will continue to anchor risk assessments, while narrative persistence metrics such as those derived from Doppelganger will remain critical signals of enforcement sufficiency. For Gulf, United States, and Israeli actors, institutional perimeter and transparency decisions will continue to constrain visibility and keep attribution confidence structurally lower. The key uncertainty is how long the current disclosure cadence can persist before it materially degrades analytical confidence in cross actor comparisons and in the measurement of AI enabled FIMI. Evidence that would significantly reduce this uncertainty includes cross platform corroboration of IUVM attribution beyond Meta, quantified data on amplification networks since January 2026, and any publication that documents AI assisted content or detection at scale.
